coredata.dc:title: Artificial intelligence-driven data generation for temperature-dependent current–voltage characteristics of diodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ersöz, Betül
Öter, Ali
Berktaş, Zeynep
Bülbül, Halil İbrahim
Di Bartolomeo, Antonio
Sağıroğlu, Şeref
Orhan, Elif
coredata.dc:description: Artificial intelligence has the potential to develop models that accurately predict the behavior of electronic devices under various operating conditions. Such models allow researchers to conduct precise performance evaluations during the design phase, reducing development time and costs. For instance, by analyzing the effects of current, voltage, and temperature on diode performance, these models can shorten the diode development cycle and promote sustainable industry growth. In this study, a nanocomposite diode based on lanthanum-doped polyethyleneimine-functionalized graphene quantum dots was fabricated to investigate the impact of temperature on diode performance. The diode's current–voltage characteristics were measured experimentally over a temperature range of 77–400 K. These measurements were used to train machine learning algorithms. Specifically, K-Nearest Neighbors, Decision Trees, and Gradient Boosting were employed to predict current–voltage characteristics at temperatures lacking experimental data. The performance of these models was evaluated using metrics such as the coefficient of determination, mean squared error, and mean absolute error. Among the models, Gradient Boosting demonstrated the highest accuracy, achieving a coefficient of determination of 0.9998, a mean squared error of 0.0026, and a mean absolute error of 0.0222, though accuracy varied with temperature. To test the accuracy of the predicted values, experimental measurements were repeated for the corresponding temperatures, confirming the model's performance. The findings indicate that artificial intelligence-assisted, temperature-dependent data generation can enhance the development of a sustainable diode industry by reducing energy consumption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Artificial intelligence
KNN
Decision trees
Gradient boosting
GQDs-based diode characteristics

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flowchart of model training and testing procedures.
R2 performances for KNN, DT, and GB models in different temperatures.
MSE and MAE performances for KNN, DT, and GB models in different temperatures.

(a) ln(I)–V plots for experimental and AI-based models at 150 K. (b) ln(I)–V graphs of experimental and predicted values with a +2 increment at 150 K from the utilized models. (c) ln(I)–V graphs of experimental and predicted values with a +2 increment at 125 K from the utilized models. (d) ln(I)–V graphs of experimental and predicted values with a +2 increment at 300 K from the utilized models. (e) ln(I)–V graphs of experimental and predicted values with a +2 increment at 350 K from the utilized models.
Experimental and predicted diode parameters as a function of temperature.
Temperature-dependent I–V graph at r 150 K with a + 0.002 increment per step.
Temperature-dependent I–V graph at 250 K with a + 0.002 increment per step.
Temperature-dependent I–V graph at 375 K with a + 0.002 increment per step.
(a) The linear region of the experimental ln(I)–V graph at 100 K. (b) The linear region of the predicted ln(I)–V graph at 100 K.

row.entry:
n_neighbors: 3, 5, 7, 9
Weights: Uniform, distance
Metric: Euclidean, Manhattan

max_dept: None, 10, 20, 30
min_samples_split: 2, 5, 10
min_samples_leaf: 1, 2, 4
max_features: None, sqrt, log2
random_state:42

n_estimator: 100, 200, 300
max_depth: 3, 5, 7
min_samples_split: 2, 5, 10
min_samples_leaf: 1, 2, 4
subsample: 0.8, 1.0
learning_rate: 0.01, 0.05, 0.1
random_state:42

n_estimators:300
learning_rate: 0.1
max_depth:7
min_samples_split:2
min_samples_leaf:2
subsample:0.8

Comparisons for models' performances and parameters of diodes in the literature.

head.ce:title: Artificial intelligence-driven data generation for temperature-dependent current–voltage characteristics of diodes

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Artificial intelligence has the potential to develop models that accurately predict the behavior of electronic devices under various operating conditions. Such models allow researchers to conduct precise performance evaluations during the design phase, reducing development time and costs. For instance, by analyzing the effects of current, voltage, and temperature on diode performance, these models can shorten the diode development cycle and promote sustainable industry growth. In this study, a nanocomposite diode based on lanthanum-doped polyethyleneimine-functionalized graphene quantum dots was fabricated to investigate the impact of temperature on diode performance. The diode's current–voltage characteristics were measured experimentally over a temperature range of 77–400 K. These measurements were used to train machine learning algorithms. Specifically, K-Nearest Neighbors, Decision Trees, and Gradient Boosting were employed to predict current–voltage characteristics at temperatures lacking experimental data. The performance of these models was evaluated using metrics such as the coefficient of determination, mean squared error, and mean absolute error. Among the models, Gradient Boosting demonstrated the highest accuracy, achieving a coefficient of determination of 0.9998, a mean squared error of 0.0026, and a mean absolute error of 0.0222, though accuracy varied with temperature. To test the accuracy of the predicted values, experimental measurements were repeated for the corresponding temperatures, confirming the model's performance. The findings indicate that artificial intelligence-assisted, temperature-dependent data generation can enhance the development of a sustainable diode industry by reducing energy consumption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The trained model files and associated code will be made available as open source to ensure reproducibility and can be accessed at https://github.com/alioter71/.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous development of technology, the field of application of diodes will continue to expand. As in all fields, experimental studies in this area can be accelerated by using artificial intelligence (AI). AI is exploited to create models to predict how diodes perform under different operating conditions. By analyzing the effects of factors such as current, voltage, and temperature on diode performance, these models help scientists make more accurate performance assessments at the design stage, reducing the time and cost of subsequent testing. Thus, the development cycle can be significantly shortened by using AI to generate various diode structures and predict their electrical performance automatically. Applying AI can also help the diode industry to become sustainable.

Only a few studies have used AI-based algorithms to predict the parameters of Schottky diodes. Millman et al. [1] explored the feasibility of using machine learning (ML) techniques to extract Schottky diode parameters from current-voltage (I–V) and capacitance–voltage (C–V) characteristic curves. To analyze and extract the internal parameters of a Schottky photodiode, artificial neural network (ANN) based models were proposed by Ocaya et al. [2]. The results showed the effective learning capabilities of the ANN models in accurately capturing the photodiodes' photo responses and predicting the Schottky diodes' internal parameters. To optimize multi-terminal nanoelectronic devices, Ruiz Euler et al. [3] used deep neural networks (DNNs). The Au|GaN|GaAs Schottky diode optimal parameters were estimated with advanced swarm intelligence (SI) algorithms by Rabehi et al. [4]. Öter et al. [5] employed K-Nearest Neighbour (KNN), Random Forest (RF), Multilayered Perceptron (MLP), and Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithms to predict diode parameters from I–V measurements of polyethyleneimine (PEI)-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-based Schottky hybrid diodes. The RF algorithm achieved the best performance, which gave an RMSE value of 4.1 ×
10
− 5
and an R2 value of 0.999998 among the four algorithms. An ANN model for predicting the I–V values of 6H-SiC/MEH-PPV with a polymer interface was developed by Güzel and Çolak [6]. Barkhordari et al. [7] used Gaussian Process Regression (GPR), Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR), SVR, and ANN to investigate the I–V characteristics of different Schottky-type structures with and without a nanocomposite interface. Doğan et al. [8] showed that the artificial hummingbirds' algorithm (AHA) had a higher and more symmetrical prediction performance in determining the fundamental parameters of the Schottky diode compared to other presented algorithms.

It is also essential to examine the electrical properties of diodes under different temperature conditions. In addition to determining the performance of the diode at room temperature, many studies have been conducted on determining the temperature-dependent changes in diode parameters, and it has been stated that these parameters are an essential function of temperature. In one of the AI-assisted I–V–T studies, Torun et al. [9] estimated the I–V characteristic of the Au/Ni/n-GaN/undoped GaN Schottky diode in the temperature range from 40 to 400 K by using Adaptive Neuro Fuzzy System (ANFIS), ANN, SVR, and GPR techniques. It was found that the ANFIS model performed much better than the others. Sellai et al. [10] extracted Schottky diode and solar cell parameters by fitting experimental data to theoretical curves simulated by specific physical processes using this genetic algorithm. Laajimi et al. [11] used mathematical, DT, and ANN models to predict the current output of the Ag/P3HT/SiNWs diode. The ANN method provided very accurate and fast predictions compared to other models. The electrical parameters and C–V characteristics of GaAs/AlGaAs multi-quantum well Schottky diodes at different temperatures were predicted using two ANN models developed by Garoudja et al. [12]. The results from the neural network models agreed with the experimental data. The reverse characteristics predictions of hetero-epitaxial-grown diamonds were performed using machine learning regression models by Abdelrahman et al. [13]. It is important to note that all studies reported in the literature show remarkable predictive performance using ANNs.

The primary goal of this work is to develop machine learning models that predict the temperature-dependent I–V characteristics of lanthanum-doped polyethyleneimine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (La-doped PEI-GQDs) based nanocomposite diode, thereby reducing both the time and cost associated with extensive experimental measurements. Additionally, this research aims to contribute to efforts focused on enhancing sustainability, energy efficiency, and minimizing waste in manufacturing.

The novelty of this work lies in the use of AI algorithms to predict the temperature-dependent performance of nanocomposite diodes, which can potentially reduce both the time and cost of subsequent development. AI-assisted models can accurately predict I–V characteristics without needing experimental data within the same temperature range, offering a valuable alternative to traditional experimental methods.

Another key aspect of the study is the integration of tree-based AI algorithms (K-Nearest Neighbors-KNN, Decision Trees-DT, Gradient Boosting-GB) to predict the temperature-dependent behavior of nanocomposite diodes. This approach distinguishes the study from previous works that employed various AI and ML techniques to predict diode parameters. The tree-based algorithms used in this study offer distinct advantages, including their understandable structure, flexibility, minimal preprocessing requirements, and firm performance across various machine learning problems. Unlike more complex ANNs, these models provide faster solutions and require less computational power.

Many AI models with different architectures, algorithms, and techniques have been used to solve problems in various fields [14,15]. In AI, optimal forecasting is often defined as combining algorithms, modeling, and optimization techniques for better outcomes. In this study, AI was employed to investigate the effect of temperature on Schottky diode performance by training models on experimental data and estimating diode parameters for temperatures where no experimental data were available. For the experimental part of the study, a nanocomposite diode based on lanthanum (La)-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was fabricated. In synthesizing the nanocomposite solution, PEI-functionalized GQDs were first synthesized using the environmentally friendly hydrothermal method. Subsequently, La(NO3)3 was added to the solution, leading to the formation of La(OH)3 nanoparticles within the GQDs-based solution, resulting in the La(OH)3 nanoparticle-doped GQDs nanocomposite. This solution was then spin-coated onto an n-type silicon (100) wafer (1–10 Ωcm, 350 μm thickness) to form a 30 nm thin film. After the coating process, gold (Au) contacts were created using a sputtering technique. The detailed production stages of the fabricated nanocomposite diode are described elsewhere [16].

In this study, I–V characteristics of the La-doped PEI-GQDs-based diode were obtained at eight different temperature points (77, 200, 293, 320, 340, 360, 380, and 400 K) using a Semiconductor Analyzer Keithley 4200 and were used to train AI algorithms. Ten different temperatures were selected for testing (100, 125, 150, 175, 225, 250, 275, 300, 350, and 375 K). AI-assisted KNN, DT, and GB machine learning algorithms were employed to generate the test data's ln(I)–V characteristics—the input voltage changes in 0.01 V intervals in the datasets, with 1000 data points for each temperature. Predictions were made for the corresponding ln(I) values using ML techniques, and the performance of the algorithms was evaluated with statistical metrics such as R2 (R-squared), MSE (Mean Squared Error), MAE (Mean Absolute Error), and MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error). A detailed flowchart of the study is provided in Fig. 1 .

This study employed three ML algorithms to predict the temperature-dependent I–V characteristics of a La-doped PEI-GQDs Schottky nanocomposite diode. These algorithms KNN, DT, and GB—were selected for their distinct advantages and performance in predicting diode parameters under varying conditions. Each model provides unique simplicity, interpretability, and accuracy strengths, making them well-suited for the task.

K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN): KNN is a straightforward yet powerful algorithm that performs classification or regression based on the proximity to the nearest k neighbors in the training data. Using distance metrics such as Euclidean or Manhattan distance, KNN predicts the target value based on the majority class or weighted average of the closest data points. Its simplicity and effectiveness make it an ideal choice for applications with relatively small datasets [17].

Euclidean Distance is defined as:
(1)
d
x y
=
∑
i = 1
n
x
i −
y i
2
where x and y are two n-dimensional data points, and n is the number of features. This formula calculates the straight-line distance between the points in multi-dimensional space. For classification, KNN predicts the class of a test data point by considering the majority class among its k nearest neighbors, while for regression, it calculates the weighted average of the closest neighbors.

Decision Trees (DT): A DT is a nonlinear model that splits the data into subsets based on specific features to make predictions. It uses entropy, information gain (IG), and Gini Index to decide the best splits at each node [17,18].

Entropy (H) is a measure of uncertainty in a dataset. It is defined as:
(2)
H
S
= −
∑
i = 1
n
p i
log 2
p i
where pi is the probability of class i. A lower entropy value indicates that the data is more homogeneous (or pure), and the decision tree can split it more effectively.

IG measures the reduction in entropy achieved by splitting the dataset based on a particular feature:
(3)
IG
S A
= H
S
−
∑
v ∈ Values
A
S v
S
H
S v
where S is the entire dataset, A is the feature selected for splitting, and S ν
is the subset of the data where feature A takes the value ν.

Gini Index is another measure used to evaluate splits:
(4)
Gini
S
= 1 −
∑
i = 1
n
p i 2
The lower the Gini value, the better the data is partitioned. DT choose the split based on the highest information gain or the lowest Gini index.

Gradient Boosting (GB): GB is an ensemble learning technique that builds a series of weak learners (typically decision trees) iteratively. Each new tree tries to correct the errors made by the previous ones, making the final model a strong predictor [19,20].

The estimated value for a data point in the t-th iteration is given by:
(5)
F t
x
=
F
t − 1
X
+ γ
h t
X
where:

F t
x
t is the predicted value at iteration,

F
t − 1
X
is the predicted value from the previous iteration,

h t
X
is the weak model (usually a decision tree) added at iteration t,
γ is the learning rate, controlling how much each weak model contributes to the final prediction.
The learning process focuses on correcting the mistakes made by previous trees in the ensemble, effectively improving the model's accuracy with each iteration.
This study trained the models with specific hyper-parameter settings to ensure optimal performance and prevent overfitting. The selected parameters for each model are as follows:
KNN: The number of neighbors (k) was set to 3, which controls the number of nearest neighbors considered during classification.

Decision Tree (DT): The hyperparameters were set as follows: max_depth = 20, which limits the depth of the tree; min_samples_split = 2, allowing the smallest possible split; and min_samples_leaf = 1, ensuring that each leaf node contains at least one sample.

Gradient Boosting (GB): The hyperparameters were set as follows: n_estimators = 300 (the number of boosting stages), learning_rate = 0.1 (the step size at each iteration), max_depth = 7 (the depth of each individual tree), min_samples_split = 2, min_samples_leaf = 2, and subsample = 0.8 (the fraction of samples used for fitting each base learner).

The hyperparameters for the AI models (KNN, DT, and GB) were selected using the GridSearchCV method, which systematically explores a predefined hyperparameter grid to identify the optimal combination [21,22].

This approach evaluates the model's performance for each combination of hyperparameters using cross-validation, ensuring the best-performing parameters are selected. Specifically, for KNN, hyperparameters such as the number of neighbors (k) and the distance metric were optimized. At the same time, parameters like the maximum depth and the minimum samples required to split a node were fine-tuned for DT.

These parameters were chosen based on previous literature, practical experience, and the results of preliminary experiments. The selected hyperparameter values and their corresponding roles are detailed in Table 1 . Additionally, to validate model performance, we applied k-fold cross-validation with k = 5, which helps prevent overfitting and ensures robust model evaluation.

While no specific mathematical formulas were directly used for hyperparameter selection, the GridSearchCV method and cross-validation process allowed us to systematically optimize each model's parameters to ensure the best performance. For Gradient Boosting, the maximum number of iterations (or cycles) was set to n_estimators (300), while for KNN and DT, convergence was mainly influenced by the model's structure, such as the number of neighbors or tree depth.

Evaluation criteria: Using evaluation metrics to assess model performance allows us to understand and improve a model's predictive power before deploying it with new data. In this study, four commonly used error metrics—R2, MSE, MAE, and MAPE—were employed to evaluate the performance of a regression model. MSE and MAE calculate the deviation between the predicted and actual values of the model, but they differ in how they treat these deviations. MSE squares the errors before averaging, which penalizes more significant errors more heavily, making it sensitive to outliers. MAE, on the other hand, takes the average of the absolute errors, treating all errors equally and providing a more straightforward indication of average prediction error [5,23], R2 measures the proportion of variance in the dependent variable that is explained by the independent variables in the model. It indicates how well the model fits the data, with values closer to 1 indicating a better fit [23,24]. MAPE was also included to assess the model's performance regarding percentage errors. It calculates the average of the absolute percentage errors between predicted and actual values, making helpful in understanding the model's accuracy in relative terms [25]. MAPE is especially valuable when comparing models across different datasets, as it standardizes the error in percentage terms, irrespective of the scale of the dependent variable. These metrics were chosen to comprehensively assess model accuracy, considering the magnitude and relative size of errors and the model's overall fit to the data. Below, the mathematical representations of the R2, MSE, MAE, and MAPE metrics are provided.
(6)
R 2
= 1 −
∑ i N
Y
predict
i
−
Y i
2
∑ i N
Y i
−
Y mean
2
(7)
MSE =
1 N
∑ i N
Y i
−
Y
predict
i
2
(8)
MAE =
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
Y
predict
i
−
Y mean
(9)
MAPE =
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
Y i
−
Y
predict
i
Y i
x
100
where:

Y i
: Actual value,
Y
predict
i
: Value predicted by the model,
Y mean
: Mean of the actual values, N: Number of data points.

This study used the I–V measurements of a La-doped PEI-GQDs-based nanocomposite diode to train and test three different ML models (KNN, DT, and GB) at varying temperatures. A total of 1000 ln(I)–V data points were collected for each temperature value, with the voltage (V) ranging from −5 V to +5 V in 0.01 V increments, while the ln(I) values were experimentally measured and calculated for each temperature.

Four widely used error metrics—R2, MSE, MAE, and MAPE—were employed to assess the regression models' performance, comprehensively evaluating the models' accuracy and predictive power across different temperature conditions. These error rates in KNN, DT, and GB Models are presented in Table 2 . According to Table 2, this study found that for lower temperatures (100 – 250 K), the MSE values for all models were generally low, with the KNN model showing the lowest error rates compared to DT and GB. As the temperature increased (especially at 325 and 350 K), MSE values rose, indicating that the models made more errors at higher temperature levels. Throughout the study, all models exhibited high R2 values, mostly around 0.999, as the temperature increased. However, at 325 K, the R2 value decreased to 0.984, showing that the model's fit to the data weakened at higher temperatures. The study revealed that the KNN model generally had lower MAE values, indicating more accurate predictions than DT and GB. At higher temperatures, MAE values increased, suggesting a decline in model accuracy, with the most significant rise observed around 325 K. Lower MAPE values indicate more accurate models. Throughout the study, KNN, DT, and GB models exhibited low MAPE values, but as the temperature increased, these values rose. This supports the observation that the models' accuracy decreases with higher temperatures.

In conclusion, at lower temperatures (100 – 250 K), the performance of the models was consistently high and stable. The KNN model had lower MSE, MAE, and MAPE values, indicating it performed better in these temperature ranges. However, as the temperature increased (significantly above 300 K), a noticeable degradation in model performance occurred. This suggests higher temperatures negatively impacted the models' accuracy, resulting in more significant prediction errors.

The other two models also performed exceptionally well. Fig. 2 shows the R2 performance for KNN, DT, and GB models at different temperatures. The MSE and MAE performances for KNN, DT, and GB models at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 3 .

Fig. 2 illustrates the R2 performance of three machine learning models—KNN, DT, and GB—across different temperature values. The results indicate that all models achieve consistently high R2 values, approaching 1.0, which signifies an excellent fit between the predicted and actual values. When comparing the models, the differences in performance are minimal. However, the GB model exhibits the highest overall R2 values, followed closely by DT and KNN. While all three models perform well, KNN shows a slight decrease in performance at higher temperatures, particularly above 350 K. Despite this minor variation, the overall trend suggests that the models maintain stable predictive accuracy across the temperature range. The results further demonstrate that temperature variations have a negligible effect on the models' predictive performance, highlighting their robustness. The slight decline observed in KNN at higher temperatures suggests that tree-based models such as DT and GB may be more resilient in capturing temperature-dependent variations. In conclusion, machine learning models effectively predict temperature-dependent characteristics with high accuracy. While GB achieves the most consistent performance, selecting the optimal model should consider computational efficiency and generalization capability.

Fig. 3 presents the MSE and MAE performances of the KNN, DT, and GB models across different temperature values. The chart provides a comparative analysis of the models' predictive errors, offering insights into their accuracy and reliability. The results indicate that the overall MSE and MAE values remain relatively low across most temperature points, confirming the models' strong predictive performances. Among the models, KNN exhibits slightly lower MSE values than DT and GB at various temperatures, suggesting a marginal advantage in reducing squared errors. However, the MAE values for all models remain closely aligned, implying that the absolute prediction errors are consistently minor. A notable deviation is observed at approximately 350 K, where the DT model experiences a sharp increase in both MSE and MAE, indicating a significant decline in prediction accuracy at this specific temperature. This suggests that the DT model might be more sensitive to variations in temperature-dependent characteristics compared to KNN and GB. The performance of KNN and GB remains relatively stable across all temperature points, reinforcing their robustness in capturing the underlying patterns. Overall, the analysis in Fig. 3 demonstrates that all three models achieve satisfactory predictive accuracy, with KNN and GB displaying greater consistency across different temperatures. The sudden performance drop in DT at higher temperatures suggests potential overfitting or instability in the model's response to temperature-dependent variations.

The KNN-MAE value at 350 K is believed to stem from experimental measurement errors, such as voltage fluctuations or sudden changes in lighting conditions, rather than issues related to the ML models. If the error were inherent to the model, similar deviations would be expected at other temperatures, such as 300 or 375 K. However, since the discrepancy is observed solely at 350 K, it highlights that the inconsistency is likely due to specific experimental conditions rather than any shortcomings of the model. Therefore, it is concluded that the observed deviation arises not from the model's performance but from environmental factors during measurements at 350 K.

The experimentally obtained ln(I) results and the predicted values using the ML method were also evaluated graphically. Based on the evaluation criteria of the predictions, the experimental values and predicted values for the diode at typically 150 K were compared through ln(I)–V curves, as shown in Fig. 4a. The experimental and predicted ln(I)–V curves for the diode at 125, 150, 300, and 350 K are presented in Fig. 4(b)–(e), with the predicted values shown with a +2 increment to enhance their distinguishability.
The calculated values for slope, intercept, ideality factor (n), and Schottky barrier (ΦB) [26] for these regions were computed for each temperature and presented in Table 3 .

The ideality factor and barrier height predictions made using the GB method were compared with experimental data across various temperature ranges in Fig. 5 . The percentage differences between the experimentally obtained and AI-predicted values of the ideality factor and barrier height have been calculated. At low temperatures (100–125 K), the predicted ideality factor values closely match the experimental ones, with a difference of approximately 3 %. As the temperature increases to the intermediate range (150–225 K), the difference becomes more significant, reaching up to 17 %. The difference decreases again at higher temperatures (250 K and above) between 1.4 % and 10.4 %. For the barrier height, the differences vary between 1.2 % and 13.3 % at lower temperatures (100−200 K). The discrepancy increases significantly in the intermediate range (250–300 K), reaching 25 %. The difference becomes even more pronounced at the highest temperatures (325–375 K), ranging between 27.9 % and 33.6 %.

These differences can be attributed to several factors. At low temperatures, AI models provide predictions that closely align with experimental values, suggesting that the training data generalizes better in this range or that physical effects are more stable. At intermediate temperatures, the AI model may struggle to accurately capture the carrier transport mechanisms, leading to deviations of up to 17 % in the ideality factor and 13 % in the barrier height. At high temperatures, additional physical effects, such as quantum mechanical tunneling and recombination processes, may not be adequately represented in the model, resulting in errors exceeding 30 %. Moreover, changes in charge transport mechanisms at higher temperatures might not be fully accounted for in the AI predictions.

AI models capture the overall trend effectively, but the differences become more pronounced at intermediate and high temperatures. The predictions slightly under-predict the ideality factor and over-predict the barrier height in these regions, probably due to limitations in fully accounting for temperature-dependent transport mechanisms. While the AI model remains a useful predictive tool, experimental values are essential in training more reliable and accurate AI models. As a result, training better models with additional experimental data and incorporating parameters that better represent the underlying physical mechanisms are necessary to improve the accuracy of AI models. Further analysis of significant deviations in barrier height estimates at higher temperatures will enhance the reliability of the AI models.

In this study, temperature-dependent I–V graphs for three different temperatures are also presented comparatively for three models. Figs. 6–8
illustrate the relationship between current (I) and voltage (V) at typically different temperatures: 150, 250, and 375 K, respectively. The experimental data is represented in blue, while the predictions from KNN, DT, and GB models are shown in red, purple, and cyan, respectively. The predicted values are shown with a + 0.002 increment to make them more distinguishable.

In Fig. 6, the experimental data follows a nonlinear trend, with current increasing significantly at higher voltages. Among the models, DT and KNN provide predictions that closely match the experimental data, whereas the GB model slightly overestimates the current, particularly in the positive voltage region. All models maintain a consistent deviation from the experimental data at lower voltages.

In Fig. 7, the current values are higher than 150 K, indicating the expected thermal activation of charge carriers. While DT and KNN continue to provide predictions close to the experimental data, the GB model tends to overestimate the current, particularly at positive voltages. Additionally, all models show some deviation in the negative voltage region, suggesting that their predictive capabilities may need further refinement under these conditions.

In Fig. 8, the current values are significantly higher than in the previous cases, which is consistent with the temperature-dependent behavior of semiconductor materials. Notably, the predictive accuracy of all three models improves, with their estimates aligning more closely with the experimental data. This suggests that the models become more accurate in approximating experimental behavior as temperature increases, reducing discrepancies observed at lower temperatures.

These findings indicate that while all three machine learning models can effectively predict the I–V characteristics, the GB model tends to overestimate the current at lower temperatures. In contrast to DT and KNN provide more consistent predictions across different temperatures. The increasing accuracy of all models at higher temperatures (375 K) suggests that temperature-dependent charge transport mechanisms may become more predictable at elevated temperatures. Further analysis of model performance at negative voltages and lower temperatures could help refine predictions and enhance overall reliability.

For each temperature value, voltage values within the -5 V to -5 V range, consisting of 1000 rows, were analyzed using a 10-step window with a 0.01 V interval. The best linear region of ln(I) values was identified through linear regression. Similarly, the GB model, which showed the best performance, was applied to determine the best linear regions. This adjustment determined the slope and intercept of the diode forward current for the linear regions. The experimental ln(I) values at 100 K. The ln(I) values predicted by the GB model within the linear region (0.40–0.31 V range with 0.01 V intervals) are shown in Fig. 9 . The linear prediction range in the first region of the diode was determined using linear regression. The corresponding equation for the 100 K data is provided in Fig. 9a and b. In the experimental data, the maximum slope region was between (0.40–0.31 V), with a slope of 32.3837. In the GB prediction, the maximum slope region was between (0.40–0.31 V), with a slope of 33.3977.

Table 4 shows a comparative study for evaluating the performances of available models in the literature and summarizes the I–V characterization data of different Schottky diode structures and the performance of the corresponding machine learning models. Each row presents the specific diode type, the dataset used, the applied model type, the hyperparameters used during training, and the performance metrics.

The study employs various machine learning algorithms, including ANN, RF, and GB. The dataset size significantly influences model performance. For instance, the La-doped PEI-GQDs-based Schottky diode was trained using a large dataset containing 18.000 data points, with the GB model applied. The optimization processes differ for each applied algorithm. ANN models involve tuning parameters such as learning rate and activation functions.

In contrast, GB models require fine-tuning of decision tree depth (max_depth), learning rate (learning_rate), sampling parameters, and other hyperparameters to enhance performance. Model accuracy is evaluated using MSE, MAE, and the coefficient of determination (R
2 ). The highest accuracy was obtained for the La-doped PEI-GQDs-based Schottky diode, with an R
2 value of 0.999. The low MSE (0.0026) and MAE (0.0217) values indicate strong predictive performance. The GB model applied to the La-doped PEI-GQDs-based Schottky diode demonstrated significantly high accuracy compared to ANN and RF models used in previous studies. This suggests the model's strong generalization capability in I–V characterization.

In conclusion, this study highlights the effectiveness of ML techniques in Schottky diode characterization. The high performance achieved for the La-doped PEI-GQDs-based Schottky diode indicates the model selection accuracy and hyperparameter tuning. Further improvements could be explored by incorporating larger datasets and more complex model architectures.

To investigate the effect of temperature on diode performance, a La-doped PEI-GQDs-based nanocomposite diode was prepared in this study. The I–V characteristics of the diode were experimentally measured for temperatures ranging from 77 to 400 K. These experimental measurements were used to train AI algorithms, specifically KNN, DT, and GB, to generate estimated I–V characteristics for different temperatures, where no experimental data were available. The performance of the algorithms was assessed using statistical metrics, including R2, MSE, MAE, and MAPE. Additional experimental measurements were conducted at the relevant temperatures to evaluate the accuracy of the test values. The estimation accuracy was outstanding and consistent with the results across all experimental datasets. Among the algorithms, the GB model performed best with R2 = 0.9998, MSE = 0.0026, and MAE = 0.0222, making it the top-performing model. Traditional methods require significant time, energy, and material consumption, as well as experienced researchers, for accurate data collection. AI-based temperature-dependent data generation can increase efficiency and sustainability in diode research by reducing the need for extensive experimental measurements. AI models such as GB provide reliable predictions, minimizing human error and the need for repeated training of new researchers. This approach speeds up development, optimizes resource utilization, and can support a more standardized and reproducible methodology for diode characterization.
The study does not report on or involve animal or human data or tissue use.
This research received no specific grant from any funding agency in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Betül Ersöz: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ali Öter: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zeynep Berktaş: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Halil İbrahim Bülbül: Validation, Software, Data curation. Antonio Di Bartolomeo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Şeref Sağıroğlu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Project administration, Data curation, Conceptualization. Elif Orhan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.
